subject: math_aa
title: Geometric Distribution CDF
stem_template: The random variable \\(X\\) follows a geometric distribution with parameter \\(p = {{p}}\\). Find \\(P(X \\leq {{k}})\\).
mark_scheme_template: The CDF of the geometric distribution is \\(P(X \\leq k) = 1-(1-p)^k\\). Substituting \\(p={{p}}\\) and \\(k={{k}}\\): \\(P(X \\leq {{k}}) = 1-(1-{{p}})^{{{k}}}\\).
hints: The geometric distribution counts trials until the first success.
The CDF is the complement of the probability that all first k trials are failures.

subject: math_aa
title: Negative Binomial Mean
stem_template: The random variable \\(X\\) follows a negative binomial distribution representing the number of trials needed to achieve \\(r = {{r}}\\) successes, where the probability of success is \\(p = {{p}}\\). Find \\(E(X)\\).
mark_scheme_template: For a negative binomial distribution with parameters \\(r\\) and \\(p\\), the mean is \\(E(X)=\\dfrac{r}{p}\\). With \\(r={{r}}\\) and \\(p={{p}}\\): \\(E(X)=\\dfrac{{{r}}}{{{p}}}\\).
hints: Recall the mean of the negative binomial distribution.
The negative binomial extends the geometric distribution to r successes.

subject: math_aa
title: PGF of Geometric Distribution
stem_template: The random variable \\(X \\sim \\text{Geo}(p)\\) with \\(p={{p}}\\). The probability generating function is \\(G_X(t) = \\dfrac{pt}{1-(1-p)t}\\). Find \\(G_X'(1)\\), the mean of \\(X\\).
mark_scheme_template: Differentiate \\(G_X(t)=\\dfrac{{{p}}t}{1-(1-{{p}})t}\\) using the quotient rule. At \\(t=1\\): \\(G_X'(1)=E(X)=\\dfrac{1}{p}=\\dfrac{1}{{{p}}}\\).
hints: Differentiate G(t) with respect to t using the quotient rule.
Evaluate at t=1 to find the mean.

subject: math_aa
title: PGF to Find Mean and Variance
stem_template: A random variable \\(X\\) has probability generating function \\(G_X(t) = \\left(\\dfrac{{{p}}t}{1-(1-{{p}})t}\\right)^{{{r}}}\\). (a) Find \\(E(X)\\). (b) Find \\(\\text{Var}(X)\\).
mark_scheme_template: (a) \\(E(X)=G_X'(1)\\). Differentiating and evaluating at \\(t=1\\) gives \\(E(X)=\\dfrac{r}{p}=\\dfrac{{{r}}}{{{p}}}\\). (b) \\(\\text{Var}(X)=G_X''(1)+G_X'(1)-[G_X'(1)]^2=\\dfrac{r(1-p)}{p^2}=\\dfrac{{{r}}(1-{{p}})}{{{p}}^2}\\).
hints: Use E(X) = G'(1) for the mean.
This PGF represents a negative binomial distribution.

subject: math_aa
title: Variance of Linear Combination
stem_template: Independent random variables \\(X\\) and \\(Y\\) have \\(\\text{Var}(X)={{vx}}\\) and \\(\\text{Var}(Y)={{vy}}\\). Find \\(\\text{Var}({{a}}X - {{b}}Y)\\).
mark_scheme_template: For independent variables, \\(\\text{Var}(aX-bY)=a^2\\text{Var}(X)+b^2\\text{Var}(Y)={{a}}^2\\cdot{{vx}}+{{b}}^2\\cdot{{vy}}\\).
hints: For independent RVs, Var(aX ± bY) = a²Var(X) + b²Var(Y).
Subtraction does NOT make the variances subtract — they always add.

subject: math_aa
title: Expectation of Linear Combination
stem_template: Random variables \\(X\\) and \\(Y\\) have \\(E(X)={{ex}}\\) and \\(E(Y)={{ey}}\\). Find \\(E({{a}}X + {{b}}Y - {{c}})\\).
mark_scheme_template: Using linearity of expectation: \\(E({{a}}X+{{b}}Y-{{c}})={{a}}E(X)+{{b}}E(Y)-{{c}}={{a}}\\cdot{{ex}}+{{b}}\\cdot{{ey}}-{{c}}\\).
hints: Expectation is linear: E(aX + bY) = aE(X) + bE(Y).
Constants shift the expectation directly.

subject: math_aa
title: Standard Deviation of Combination
stem_template: Independent random variables \\(X_1, X_2, \\ldots, X_{{n}}\\) each have mean \\({{mu}}\\) and standard deviation \\({{sigma}}\\). Find the standard deviation of \\(\\bar{X} = \\dfrac{X_1+\\cdots+X_{{n}}}{{{n}}}\\).
mark_scheme_template: \\(\\text{Var}(\\bar{X})=\\dfrac{n\\sigma^2}{n^2}=\\dfrac{\\sigma^2}{n}\\). So \\(\\text{SD}(\\bar{X})=\\dfrac{\\sigma}{\\sqrt{n}}=\\dfrac{{{sigma}}}{\\sqrt{{{n}}}}\\).
hints: Variance of a sum of independent variables equals the sum of variances.
Dividing by n affects variance by factor 1/n².

subject: math_aa
title: Covariance and Correlation from Joint Distribution
stem_template: Random variables \\(X\\) and \\(Y\\) have \\(E(X)={{ex}}\\), \\(E(Y)={{ey}}\\), \\(E(XY)={{exy}}\\), \\(\\text{Var}(X)={{vx}}\\), \\(\\text{Var}(Y)={{vy}}\\). (a) Find \\(\\text{Cov}(X,Y)\\). (b) Find the correlation coefficient \\(r_{XY}\\).
mark_scheme_template: (a) \\(\\text{Cov}(X,Y)=E(XY)-E(X)E(Y)={{exy}}-{{ex}}\\cdot{{ey}}\\). (b) \\(r_{XY}=\\dfrac{\\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\\sqrt{\\text{Var}(X)\\text{Var}(Y)}}\\).
hints: Cov(X,Y) = E(XY) - E(X)E(Y).
Correlation = Cov(X,Y) / [SD(X) × SD(Y)]

subject: math_aa
title: Unbiased Estimator of Mean
stem_template: A sample \\(X_1, X_2, \\ldots, X_n\\) is drawn from a population with mean \\(\\mu\\). Which of the following is an unbiased estimator of \\(\\mu\\)?
mark_scheme_template: An estimator \\(\\hat{\\theta}\\) is unbiased if \\(E(\\hat{\\theta})=\\theta\\). We have \\(E(\\bar{X})=\\mu\\), so \\(\\bar{X}\\) is unbiased. Note \\(E\\left(\\dfrac{\\sum X_i}{n+1}\\right)=\\dfrac{n\\mu}{n+1}\\neq\\mu\\).
hints: An unbiased estimator has expected value equal to the parameter.
Check E(estimator) = parameter for each option.

subject: math_aa
title: Unbiased Sample Variance
stem_template: A sample of size \\(n={{n}}\\) is taken from a population with variance \\(\\sigma^2\\). The sample variance with denominator \\(n-1\\) gives a value of \\({{sv}}\\). What is the unbiased estimate of \\(\\sigma^2\\)?
mark_scheme_template: The unbiased estimator of \\(\\sigma^2\\) is \\(s^2=\\dfrac{\\sum(x_i-\\bar{x})^2}{n-1}\\), which equals \\({{sv}}\\) as given.
hints: The unbiased sample variance uses n-1 in the denominator (Bessel's correction).
The sample variance s² is already unbiased when computed with n-1.

subject: math_aa
title: Computing Unbiased Variance from Data
stem_template: A sample of \\(n={{n}}\\) observations has \\(\\sum x_i = {{sumx}}\\) and \\(\\sum x_i^2 = {{sumx2}}\\). Find the unbiased estimate of the population variance.
hints: Use the computational formula: s² = [Σx² - (Σx)²/n] / (n-1).
Divide by n-1, not n, for the unbiased estimate.

subject: math_aa
title: Proving an Estimator is Unbiased
stem_template: Let \\(X_1, X_2, \\ldots, X_n\\) be a random sample from a distribution with mean \\(\\mu\\) and variance \\(\\sigma^2\\). (a) Show that \\(T = \\dfrac{{{a}}X_1 + {{b}}X_2}{{{a}}+{{b}}}\\) is an unbiased estimator of \\(\\mu\\). (b) Find \\(\\text{Var}(T)\\) in terms of \\(\\sigma^2\\).
hints: Apply E(aX) = aE(X) for each term.
Use Var(aX) = a²Var(X) for independent variables.

subject: math_aa
title: Sum of Independent Normal Variables
stem_template: \\(X \\sim N({{mux}}, {{vx}}^2)\\) and \\(Y \\sim N({{muy}}, {{vy}}^2)\\) are independent. What is the distribution of \\(X + Y\\)?
mark_scheme_template: For independent normals, \\(X+Y\\sim N(\\mu_X+\\mu_Y, \\sigma_X^2+\\sigma_Y^2)=N({{mux}}+{{muy}},{{vx}}^2+{{vy}}^2)\\).
hints: When combining independent normal variables, means add and variances add.
Be careful: variances add, not standard deviations.

subject: math_aa
title: Central Limit Theorem Application
stem_template: A population has mean \\(\\mu={{mu}}\\) and standard deviation \\(\\sigma={{sigma}}\\). A random sample of size \\(n={{n}}\\) is taken. By the CLT, what is the approximate distribution of the sample mean \\(\\bar{X}\\)?
mark_scheme_template: By the CLT, \\(\\bar{X}\\sim N\\!\\left(\\mu,\\dfrac{\\sigma^2}{n}\\right)=N\\!\\left({{mu}},\\dfrac{{{sigma}}^2}{{{n}}}\\right)\\) approximately for large \\(n\\).
hints: The CLT states that sample means are approximately normal for large n.
The standard error of the mean is σ/√n, giving variance σ²/n.

subject: math_aa
title: Probability for Sample Mean via CLT
stem_template: A population has mean \\({{mu}}\\) and standard deviation \\({{sigma}}\\). Find the probability that the mean of a random sample of \\({{n}}\\) observations exceeds \\({{x}}\\). Give your answer to 4 decimal places.
mark_scheme_template: \\(\\bar{X}\\sim N\\!\\left({{mu}},\\dfrac{{{sigma}}^2}{{{n}}}\\right)\\). Standardise: \\(Z=\\dfrac{\\bar{X}-{{mu}}}{{{sigma}}/\\sqrt{{{n}}}}\\). \\(P(\\bar{X}>{{x}})=P\\!\\left(Z>\\dfrac{{{x}}-{{mu}}}{{{sigma}}/\\sqrt{{{n}}}}\\right)\\).
hints: Use the CLT: X̄ ~ N(μ, σ²/n).
Standardise by subtracting μ and dividing by σ/√n.

subject: math_aa
title: Linear Combination of Normal Variables
stem_template: Independent random variables \\(X\\sim N({{mux}},{{sx}}^2)\\) and \\(Y\\sim N({{muy}},{{sy}}^2)\\). Find \\(P({{a}}X - {{b}}Y > {{c}})\\).

parts.label: a
parts.answer_template: Find mean and variance of aX - bY

mark_scheme_template: Let \\(W={{a}}X-{{b}}Y\\). \\(E(W)={{a}}\\cdot{{mux}}-{{b}}\\cdot{{muy}}\\). \\(\\text{Var}(W)={{a}}^2\\cdot{{sx}}^2+{{b}}^2\\cdot{{sy}}^2\\). Then \\(W\\sim N(E(W),\\text{Var}(W))\\) and standardise to find probability.
hints: Find the distribution of W = aX - bY first.
Variances always add (even for differences) when variables are independent.

subject: math_aa
title: Confidence Interval Interpretation
stem_template: A 95% confidence interval for a population mean is calculated as \\(({{lower}}, {{upper}})\\). Which statement is correct?
answer_template: If we repeated this procedure many times, 95% of such intervals would contain the true mean.
distractor_templates: There is a 95% probability that the true mean lies in this specific interval.
95% of the data lies between {{lower}} and {{upper}}.
The sample mean has a 95% chance of being correct.
mark_scheme_template: A confidence interval is a procedure: 95% of all confidence intervals constructed this way contain the true parameter. For this specific interval, the true mean either is or is not in it — probability is not 95%.

options_template.text: If we repeated this procedure many times, 95% of such intervals would contain the true mean.
There is a 95% probability that the true mean lies in this specific interval.
95% of the data lies between \\({{lower}}\\) and \\({{upper}}\\).
The sample mean has a 95% chance of being correct.

hints: A CI is a statement about the procedure, not about a fixed interval.
Once the interval is calculated, the parameter is either in it or not.

subject: math_aa
title: Constructing a 95% CI for Mean (known σ)
stem_template: A random sample of \\(n={{n}}\\) observations from a normal population with known \\(\\sigma={{sigma}}\\) yields \\(\\bar{x}={{xbar}}\\). Find the 95% confidence interval for the population mean. Give bounds to 2 decimal places.
mark_scheme_template: 95% CI: \\(\\bar{x}\\pm 1.96\\cdot\\dfrac{\\sigma}{\\sqrt{n}}={{xbar}}\\pm 1.96\\cdot\\dfrac{{{sigma}}}{\\sqrt{{{n}}}}\\).
hints: Use z* = 1.96 for a 95% confidence interval.
The margin of error is z* × σ/√n.

subject: math_aa
title: Required Sample Size for CI Width
stem_template: A population has known \\(\\sigma={{sigma}}\\). Find the minimum sample size \\(n\\) needed so that a 95% confidence interval for the mean has width at most \\({{w}}\\).
mark_scheme_template: Width \\(=2\\times 1.96\\times\\dfrac{\\sigma}{\\sqrt{n}}\\leq{{w}}\\). Rearranging: \\(n\\geq\\left(\\dfrac{2\\times 1.96\\times{{sigma}}}{{{w}}}\\right)^2\\). Round up to nearest integer.
hints: Width = 2 × margin of error.
Rearrange for n and always round up.

subject: math_aa
title: t-Interval for Unknown Population Variance
stem_template: A sample of \\(n={{n}}\\) observations has \\(\\bar{x}={{xbar}}\\) and unbiased sample standard deviation \\(s={{s}}\\). Construct a 95% confidence interval for the population mean, using the \\(t\\)-distribution with \\({{n}}-1\\) degrees of freedom. (Critical value: \\(t^*={{tstar}}\\)).
mark_scheme_template: Standard error \\(=\\dfrac{s}{\\sqrt{n}}=\\dfrac{{{s}}}{\\sqrt{{{n}}}}\\). 95% CI: \\({{xbar}}\\pm {{tstar}}\\times\\dfrac{{{s}}}{\\sqrt{{{n}}}}\\).
hints: Use the t-distribution when σ is unknown.
Degrees of freedom = n - 1.

subject: math_aa
title: Type I and Type II Errors
stem_template: In a hypothesis test with significance level \\(\\alpha={{alpha}}\\), which statement correctly defines a Type I error?
answer_template: Rejecting H₀ when H₀ is true, with probability α.
distractor_templates: Accepting H₀ when H₁ is true.
Rejecting H₁ when H₁ is true.
The probability of correctly rejecting H₀.
mark_scheme_template: Type I error: rejecting \\(H_0\\) when \\(H_0\\) is true. Its probability equals \\(\\alpha={{alpha}}\\). Type II error: failing to reject \\(H_0\\) when \\(H_1\\) is true.

options_template.text: Rejecting \\(H_0\\) when \\(H_0\\) is true, with probability \\({{alpha}}\\).

hints: Type I = false positive (reject a true null).
Type II = false negative (fail to reject a false null).

subject: math_aa
title: One-Sample z-Test Decision
stem_template: A one-sample \\(z\\)-test for a population mean gives a test statistic of \\(z={{z}}\\) for a two-tailed test. The significance level is \\(\\alpha=0.05\\) (critical value \\(z^*=1.96\\)). What is the correct conclusion?
answer_template: Reject H₀ if |z| > 1.96, otherwise fail to reject H₀.
distractor_templates: Always reject H₀ for any positive z.
mark_scheme_template: For a two-tailed test at \\(\\alpha=0.05\\), reject \\(H_0\\) if \\(|z|>1.96\\). Since \\(z={{z}}\\), \\(|z|={{z}}\\). Compare to 1.96 to decide.

options_template.text: Reject \\(H_0\\) if \\(|z|>1.96\\), otherwise fail to reject \\(H_0\\).

hints: Compare |z| to the critical value for two-tailed tests.
At α=0.05 two-tailed, the critical z-value is ±1.96.

subject: math_aa
title: Compute z-Test Statistic
stem_template: A random sample of \\(n={{n}}\\) from a population with known \\(\\sigma={{sigma}}\\) gives \\(\\bar{x}={{xbar}}\\). Test \\(H_0: \\mu={{mu0}}\\) against \\(H_1: \\mu\\neq{{mu0}}\\). Find the test statistic \\(z\\) to 3 significant figures.
mark_scheme_template: \\(z=\\dfrac{\\bar{x}-\\mu_0}{\\sigma/\\sqrt{n}}=\\dfrac{{{xbar}}-{{mu0}}}{{{sigma}}/\\sqrt{{{n}}}}\\).
hints: The test statistic is z = (x̄ - μ₀) / (σ/√n).
σ/√n is the standard error of the mean.

subject: math_aa
title: Full Hypothesis Test with Conclusion
stem_template: A manufacturer claims the mean lifetime of batteries is \\(\\mu_0={{mu0}}\\) hours. A sample of \\(n={{n}}\\) batteries has mean \\(\\bar{x}={{xbar}}\\) hours. The population standard deviation is \\(\\sigma={{sigma}}\\) hours. Carry out a hypothesis test at the \\({{alpha}}\\)% significance level and state your conclusion clearly.

parts.label: c
parts.answer_template: Compare to critical value and conclude

mark_scheme_template: (a) \\(H_0: \\mu={{mu0}}\\), \\(H_1: \\mu\\neq{{mu0}}\\) (two-tailed). (b) \\(z=\\dfrac{{{xbar}}-{{mu0}}}{{{sigma}}/\\sqrt{{{n}}}}\\). (c) Compare \\(|z|\\) to the critical value. State conclusion in context.
hints: State hypotheses first.
Use a two-tailed test unless the claim is directional.
State conclusion in context of the problem.

subject: math_aa
title: Pearson Correlation Coefficient Range
stem_template: A dataset of \\((x,y)\\) pairs gives Pearson's correlation coefficient \\(r={{r}}\\). Which statement best describes this?
answer_template: r = 0.92 indicates strong positive linear correlation.
distractor_templates: r = 0 means the variables are independent.
mark_scheme_template: Pearson's \\(r\\in[-1,1]\\). \\(|r|\\) near 1 indicates strong linear correlation; \\(r>0\\) is positive, \\(r<0\\) is negative. \\(r=0\\) means no linear correlation (but variables could still be dependent).

options_template.text: \\(r={{r}}\\) indicates a strong linear relationship (positive if \\(r>0\\), negative if \\(r<0\\)).

hints: Pearson's r is always between -1 and 1.
r = 0 does not imply independence, only no linear correlation.

subject: math_aa
title: Least-Squares Regression Line
stem_template: Given data: \\(n={{n}}\\), \\(\\sum x={{sx}}\\), \\(\\sum y={{sy}}\\), \\(\\sum x^2={{sx2}}\\), \\(\\sum xy={{sxy}}\\). Find the slope \\(b\\) of the least-squares regression line \\(\\hat{y}=a+bx\\).
mark_scheme_template: \\(b=\\dfrac{n\\sum xy-\\sum x\\sum y}{n\\sum x^2-(\\sum x)^2}=\\dfrac{{{n}}\\cdot{{sxy}}-{{sx}}\\cdot{{sy}}}{{{n}}\\cdot{{sx2}}-{{sx}}^2}\\).
hints: Use the formula b = [nΣxy - ΣxΣy] / [nΣx² - (Σx)²].
This minimises the sum of squared residuals.

subject: math_aa
title: Full Regression Analysis with Prediction
stem_template: For a dataset with \\(n={{n}}\\) points: \\(\\bar{x}={{xbar}}\\), \\(\\bar{y}={{ybar}}\\), \\(S_{xx}={{sxx}}\\), \\(S_{xy}={{sxy}}\\). (a) Find the equation of the regression line of \\(y\\) on \\(x\\). (b) Predict \\(y\\) when \\(x={{xnew}}\\). (c) State a reason why this prediction may be unreliable.
mark_scheme_template: (a) \\(b=\\dfrac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}=\\dfrac{{{sxy}}}{{{sxx}}}\\); \\(a=\\bar{y}-b\\bar{x}\\). Regression line: \\(\\hat{y}=a+bx\\). (b) Substitute \\(x={{xnew}}\\). (c) \\(x={{xnew}}\\) may be outside the range of observed data (extrapolation), making the prediction unreliable.
hints: Slope b = Sxy/Sxx where Sxx = Σ(xi - x̄)².
The line passes through (x̄, ȳ).
Extrapolation beyond the data range is unreliable.

subject: math_aa
title: Coefficient of Determination
stem_template: For a regression of \\(y\\) on \\(x\\), the correlation coefficient is \\(r={{r}}\\). What percentage of the variation in \\(y\\) is explained by the linear regression on \\(x\\)?
mark_scheme_template: The coefficient of determination \\(r^2={{r}}^2\\) gives the proportion of variance in \\(y\\) explained by the linear regression. This is \\({{r}}^2\\times 100\\%\\).
hints: r² (not r) gives the proportion of explained variance.
Multiply by 100 for percentage.

subject: math_aa
title: De Morgan's Law
stem_template: Which of the following correctly states De Morgan's Law?
mark_scheme_template: De Morgan's Laws: \\((A\\cup B)'=A'\\cap B'\\) and \\((A\\cap B)'=A'\\cup B'\\). The complement of a union is the intersection of complements.
hints: Think about complement of 'either' — you must be outside BOTH sets.
The complement of a union is the intersection of complements.

subject: math_aa
title: Set Cardinality with Inclusion-Exclusion
mark_scheme_template: Inclusion-exclusion: \\(|A\\cup B|=|A|+|B|-|A\\cap B|={{a}}+{{b}}-{{ab}}\\).
hints: Use the inclusion-exclusion principle.

subject: math_aa
title: Symmetric Difference
stem_template: Sets \\(A\\) and \\(B\\) satisfy \\(|A|={{a}}\\), \\(|B|={{b}}\\), \\(|A\\cap B|={{ab}}\\). Find \\(|A\\triangle B|\\) (symmetric difference).
mark_scheme_template: \\(A\\triangle B=(A\\setminus B)\\cup(B\\setminus A)\\). \\(|A\\triangle B|=|A|+|B|-2|A\\cap B|={{a}}+{{b}}-2\\cdot{{ab}}\\).
hints: Symmetric difference = elements in A or B but not both.

subject: math_aa
title: Proving Set Identity
stem_template: Prove that for any sets \\(A\\), \\(B\\), and \\(C\\): \\(A\\cap(B\\cup C)=(A\\cap B)\\cup(A\\cap C)\\).
mark_scheme_template: Let \\(x\\in A\\cap(B\\cup C)\\). Then \\(x\\in A\\) and \\(x\\in B\\cup C\\). Case 1: \\(x\\in B\\Rightarrow x\\in A\\cap B\\). Case 2: \\(x\\in C\\Rightarrow x\\in A\\cap C\\). Either way \\(x\\in(A\\cap B)\\cup(A\\cap C)\\). Reverse inclusion by similar argument.
hints: Prove both containments: LHS ⊆ RHS and RHS ⊆ LHS.
Let x be an arbitrary element of one side and show it belongs to the other.

subject: math_aa
title: Equivalence Relation Properties
stem_template: A relation \\(R\\) on \\(\\mathbb{Z}\\) is defined by \\(aRb \\Leftrightarrow {{m}} \\mid (a-b)\\). Which properties does \\(R\\) satisfy?
mark_scheme_template: Reflexive: \\({{m}}\\mid(a-a)=0\\). ✓ Symmetric: if \\({{m}}\\mid(a-b)\\) then \\({{m}}\\mid(b-a)\\). ✓ Transitive: if \\({{m}}\\mid(a-b)\\) and \\({{m}}\\mid(b-c)\\) then \\({{m}}\\mid(a-c)\\). ✓ So \\(R\\) is an equivalence relation.
hints: Check all three properties: reflexive, symmetric, transitive.
This is congruence modulo m.

subject: math_aa
title: Counting Equivalence Classes
stem_template: The relation \\(R\\) on \\(\\{1, 2, \\ldots, {{n}}\\}\\) is defined by \\(aRb\\Leftrightarrow {{m}}\\mid(a-b)\\). How many distinct equivalence classes does \\(R\\) have?
mark_scheme_template: The equivalence classes are the residue classes modulo \\({{m}}\\): \\([0],[1],\\ldots,[{{m}}-1]\\). There are exactly \\({{m}}\\) classes.
hints: The equivalence classes correspond to residues modulo m.
There are exactly m classes: [0], [1], ..., [m-1].

subject: math_aa
title: Proving a Relation is an Equivalence
stem_template: Define \\(R\\) on \\(\\mathbb{R}\\times\\mathbb{R}\\) by \\((a,b)R(c,d)\\Leftrightarrow a^2+b^2=c^2+d^2\\). (a) Prove \\(R\\) is an equivalence relation. (b) Describe the equivalence class of \\(({{x}},{{y}})\\).
mark_scheme_template: (a) Reflexive: \\(a^2+b^2=a^2+b^2\\). ✓ Symmetric: if \\(a^2+b^2=c^2+d^2\\) then vice versa. ✓ Transitive: if \\(a^2+b^2=c^2+d^2\\) and \\(c^2+d^2=e^2+f^2\\) then \\(a^2+b^2=e^2+f^2\\). ✓ (b) The class is \\(\\{(x,y): x^2+y^2={{x}}^2+{{y}}^2\\}\\), a circle of radius \\(\\sqrt{{{x}}^2+{{y}}^2}\\).
hints: Check reflexive, symmetric, transitive separately.
Each equivalence class is a circle centred at the origin.

subject: math_aa
title: Injective, Surjective, Bijective
stem_template: The function \\(f:\\mathbb{R}\\to\\mathbb{R}\\) defined by \\(f(x)=x^3\\) is:
mark_scheme_template: \\(f(x)=x^3\\) is strictly increasing on \\(\\mathbb{R}\\), so it is injective. Every real number has a real cube root, so it is surjective. Hence bijective.
hints: Injective: f(a) = f(b) implies a = b.
Surjective: every y in the codomain has a preimage.

subject: math_aa
title: Classifying a Finite Function
stem_template: A function \\(f: A\\to B\\) where \\(|A|={{a}}\\) and \\(|B|={{b}}\\). If \\(f\\) is injective and \\(|A|<|B|\\), which is true?
mark_scheme_template: If \\(f\\) is injective and \\(|A|<|B|\\), then the image of \\(f\\) has at most \\(|A|={{a}}\\) elements, leaving \\(|B|-|A|={{b}}-{{a}}\\) elements of \\(B\\) without a preimage. So \\(f\\) cannot be surjective.
hints: For a bijection, |A| must equal |B|.
If |A| < |B|, not all of B can be mapped to.

subject: math_aa
title: Proving Bijection and Finding Inverse
stem_template: Let \\(f:\\mathbb{R}\\to\\mathbb{R}\\) be defined by \\(f(x)={{a}}x+{{b}}\\). (a) Prove \\(f\\) is a bijection. (b) Find \\(f^{-1}(x)\\).
mark_scheme_template: (a) Injective: \\({{a}}x_1+{{b}}={{a}}x_2+{{b}}\\Rightarrow x_1=x_2\\). ✓ Surjective: for any \\(y\\in\\mathbb{R}\\), set \\(x=\\frac{y-{{b}}}{{{a}}}\\). ✓ (b) \\(y={{a}}x+{{b}}\\Rightarrow x=\\frac{y-{{b}}}{{{a}}}\\), so \\(f^{-1}(x)=\\frac{x-{{b}}}{{{a}}}\\).
hints: Prove injective by assuming f(a) = f(b) and showing a = b.
Prove surjective by constructing an explicit preimage for any y.

subject: math_aa
title: Binary Operation Closure
stem_template: The operation \\(\\star\\) on \\(\\mathbb{Z}\\) is defined by \\(a\\star b = a + b - {{k}}\\). Is this operation closed on \\(\\mathbb{Z}\\)?
answer_template: Yes, because a + b - k is always an integer when a, b are integers.
distractor_templates: No, because k may be greater than a or b.
No, because the result could be negative.
Yes, only if a and b are positive.
mark_scheme_template: For all \\(a, b\\in\\mathbb{Z}\\), \\(a+b-{{k}}\\in\\mathbb{Z}\\) since integers are closed under addition and subtraction. So \\(\\star\\) is closed on \\(\\mathbb{Z}\\).

options_template.text: No, because \\({{k}}\\) may be greater than \\(a\\) or \\(b\\).
No, because the result could be negative.
Yes, only if \\(a\\) and \\(b\\) are positive.

hints: Closure means the result must be in the same set.
Negative integers are still integers — closure is about set membership.

subject: math_aa
title: Operation Table Completion
stem_template: The binary operation \\(\\star\\) on \\(\\{0, 1, 2, \\ldots, {{m}}-1\\}\\) is defined by \\(a\\star b = (a+b) \\bmod {{m}}\\). Find the value of \\({{a}} \\star {{b}}\\).
hints: Add a and b, then take the remainder when divided by m.
mod m gives a value in {0, 1, ..., m-1}.

subject: math_aa
title: Cayley Table Analysis
stem_template: A binary operation \\(\\star\\) on \\(S=\\{a,b,c,d\\}\\) is given by a Cayley table. (a) Is \\(\\star\\) commutative? (b) Find the identity element, if it exists. (c) Is the operation closed?

parts.label: a
parts.answer_template: Check if table is symmetric about the main diagonal

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: Find row/column that leaves every element unchanged

parts.label: c
parts.answer_template: All entries must be in S

mark_scheme_template: (a) Commutative iff the table is symmetric about the main diagonal. (b) Identity element \\(e\\) satisfies \\(e\\star x=x\\star e=x\\) for all \\(x\\). Look for a row and column that reproduce the header. (c) Closed iff every entry in the table belongs to \\(S\\).
hints: A symmetric Cayley table indicates commutativity.
The identity element reproduces the row/column headers exactly.

subject: math_aa
title: Associativity Test
stem_template: The operation \\(\\star\\) on \\(\\mathbb{R}\\) is defined by \\(a\\star b = a + b + {{k}}ab\\). Is \\(\\star\\) associative?
distractor_templates: No — star is never associative when products appear.
No — because the operation is not commutative.
mark_scheme_template: Compute \\((a\\star b)\\star c=(a+b+{{k}}ab)+c+{{k}}(a+b+{{k}}ab)c\\). Similarly for \\(a\\star(b\\star c)\\). Expand and verify they are equal.

options_template.text: Yes — verify \\((a\\star b)\\star c=a\\star(b\\star c)\\) algebraically.
No — \\(\\star\\) is never associative when products appear.
No — because the operation is not commutative.

hints: Compute (a★b)★c and a★(b★c) and compare.
Commutativity and associativity are independent properties.

subject: math_aa
title: Verifying Distributivity
stem_template: Operations \\(\\oplus\\) and \\(\\otimes\\) on \\(\\mathbb{Z}\\) are defined by \\(a\\oplus b=a+b\\) and \\(a\\otimes b=ab\\). Verify: does \\(\\otimes\\) distribute over \\(\\oplus\\)? Find \\({{a}}\\otimes({{b}}\\oplus{{c}})\\) and \\(({{a}}\\otimes{{b}})\\oplus({{a}}\\otimes{{c}})\\).
hints: ⊗ is multiplication, ⊕ is addition.
This is just the ordinary distributive law of arithmetic.

subject: math_aa
title: Checking All Operation Properties
stem_template: The operation \\(\\star\\) on \\(\\mathbb{R}^+\\) is defined by \\(a\\star b = \\sqrt{ab}\\). Determine whether \\(\\star\\) is: (a) closed, (b) commutative, (c) associative.

parts.label: a
parts.answer_template: Yes — product of positives is positive, square root of positive is positive

parts.label: c
parts.answer_template: No — (a★b)★c = (ab)^(1/2)^(1/2)... show inequality with a counterexample

mark_scheme_template: (a) For \\(a,b>0\\): \\(\\sqrt{ab}>0\\in\\mathbb{R}^+\\). Closed. ✓ (b) \\(\\sqrt{ab}=\\sqrt{ba}\\). Commutative. ✓ (c) \\((a\\star b)\\star c=\\sqrt{\\sqrt{ab}\\cdot c}=(ab)^{1/4}c^{1/2}\\). \\(a\\star(b\\star c)=a^{1/2}(bc)^{1/4}\\). These are not equal in general. Not associative. ✗
hints: Try a specific counterexample for associativity.
a★(b★c) = √(a·√(bc)) ≠ (√(ab))★c in general.

subject: math_aa
title: Identity Element
stem_template: The operation \\(\\star\\) on \\(\\mathbb{R}\\) is defined by \\(a\\star b=a+b-{{k}}\\). Find the identity element.
mark_scheme_template: Require \\(e\\star a=a\\): \\(e+a-{{k}}=a\\Rightarrow e={{k}}\\). Verify: \\(a\\star{{k}}=a+{{k}}-{{k}}=a\\). ✓
hints: Solve e★a = a for e.
Substitute back to verify both e★a = a and a★e = a.

subject: math_aa
title: Finding Inverse Element
stem_template: The operation \\(\\star\\) on \\(\\mathbb{R}\\) is defined by \\(a\\star b=a+b-{{k}}\\) with identity \\(e={{k}}\\). Find the inverse of \\(a\\) under \\(\\star\\).
mark_scheme_template: Require \\(a\\star a'=e={{k}}\\): \\(a+a'-{{k}}={{k}}\\Rightarrow a'=2{{k}}-a\\).
hints: Solve a★a' = e where e is the identity.
Rearrange to find a'.

subject: math_aa
title: Identity and Inverses in a Group Structure
stem_template: The operation \\(\\star\\) on \\(\\mathbb{R}\\setminus\\{0\\}\\) is defined by \\(a\\star b=\\dfrac{ab}{{{k}}}\\). (a) Find the identity element. (b) Find the inverse of \\(a\\). (c) Is this a group?

parts.label: c
parts.answer_template: Yes — closed, associative, identity, inverses all exist

mark_scheme_template: (a) \\(a\\star e=\\frac{ae}{{{k}}}=a\\Rightarrow e={{k}}\\). (b) \\(a\\star a'={{k}}\\Rightarrow\\frac{aa'}{{{k}}}={{k}}\\Rightarrow a'=\\frac{{{k}}^2}{a}\\). (c) Verify all four group axioms hold.
hints: Set a★e = a to find the identity.
Set a★a' = e to find the inverse.

subject: math_aa
title: Group Axioms
stem_template: Which set of axioms defines a group \\((G, \\star)\\)?
mark_scheme_template: A group requires exactly four axioms: (G1) Closure, (G2) Associativity, (G3) Identity element, (G4) Inverses for all elements. Commutativity is NOT required (that would make it an abelian group).
hints: There are exactly 4 group axioms.
Commutativity is extra — it makes an abelian/commutative group.

subject: math_aa
title: Verifying Group Axioms
stem_template: Does \\((\\mathbb{Z}_{{n}}, +_{{n}})\\) form a group, where \\(+_n\\) denotes addition modulo \\({{n}}\\)?
answer_template: Yes — all four group axioms are satisfied.
distractor_templates: No — there is no identity element.
No — not every element has an inverse.
No — the operation is not associative.
mark_scheme_template: \\((\\mathbb{Z}_n,+_n)\\): Closed ✓, Associative ✓ (inherited from \\(\\mathbb{Z}\\)), Identity is 0 ✓, Inverse of \\(k\\) is \\(n-k\\) ✓. This is a cyclic group of order \\(n\\).

options_template.text: Yes — all four group axioms are satisfied.
No — there is no identity element.
No — not every element has an inverse.
No — the operation is not associative.

hints: The identity under addition mod n is 0.
The inverse of k is n-k (since k + (n-k) = n ≡ 0 mod n).

subject: math_aa
title: Proving a Non-Group
stem_template: The set \\(S=\\mathbb{Z}\\) under the operation \\(a\\star b=a-b\\). (a) Show that \\((\\mathbb{Z},\\star)\\) is not a group by identifying which axiom fails. (b) Find the identity element if it exists.

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: e = 0 is a right identity but not a left identity

mark_scheme_template: (a) \\((a\\star b)\\star c=(a-b)-c=a-b-c\\). \\(a\\star(b\\star c)=a-(b-c)=a-b+c\\). These differ when \\(c\\neq 0\\). So associativity fails. (b) \\(a\\star 0=a-0=a\\) (right identity), but \\(0\\star a=0-a=-a\\neq a\\). No (two-sided) identity exists.
hints: Test associativity with specific numbers first.
Check if a★e = e★a = a for some fixed e.

subject: math_aa
title: Order of a Group
stem_template: The group \\((\\mathbb{Z}_{{{n}}}, +)\\) has order \\({{n}}\\). What is the order of the element \\({{a}}\\) in this group?
mark_scheme_template: The order of element \\(a\\) in \\(\\mathbb{Z}_n\\) is \\(\\dfrac{n}{\\gcd(a,n)}\\).
hints: The order of a is the smallest positive k such that a+a+...+a (k times) = 0 mod n.
This equals n/gcd(a,n).

subject: math_aa
title: Symmetry Group of a Triangle
stem_template: The symmetry group of an equilateral triangle has 6 elements. (a) List all 6 symmetries (rotations and reflections). (b) Show it is non-abelian with an example. (c) What is the order of each element?

parts.label: a
parts.answer_template: e, r, r², s, sr, sr² where r = 120° rotation, s = reflection

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: Find r,s such that rs ≠ sr

parts.label: c
parts.answer_template: e: order 1, r,r²: order 3, s,sr,sr²: order 2

mark_scheme_template: (a) \\(e, r, r^2\\) (rotations by \\(0°, 120°, 240°\\)) and \\(s, sr, sr^2\\) (reflections). (b) \\(rs\\neq sr\\) in general. (c) \\(|e|=1\\), \\(|r|=|r^2|=3\\), \\(|s|=|sr|=|sr^2|=2\\). This is the dihedral group \\(D_3\\cong S_3\\).
hints: An equilateral triangle has 3 rotational and 3 reflective symmetries.
Dihedral group D₃ is the simplest non-abelian group.

subject: math_aa
title: Order of Element in Multiplication Group
stem_template: In the group \\((\\mathbb{Z}_{{p}}^*, \\times)\\) where \\(p={{p}}\\) is prime, find the order of the element \\({{a}}\\).
answer_template: smallest k such that a^k ≡ 1 mod p
mark_scheme_template: Compute successive powers of \\({{a}}\\) modulo \\({{p}}\\) until you reach 1. The order divides \\(p-1\\) by Lagrange's theorem.
hints: Compute a¹, a², a³, ... mod p until you get 1.
The order must divide p-1 by Lagrange's theorem.

subject: math_aa
title: Generator of a Cyclic Group
stem_template: Which elements are generators of \\(\\mathbb{Z}_{{n}}\\)?
mark_scheme_template: An element \\(a\\in\\mathbb{Z}_n\\) is a generator iff \\(\\gcd(a,n)=1\\). The number of generators equals \\(\\phi(n)\\) (Euler's totient).
hints: A generator has order equal to the group order n.
Order of a in Zₙ is n/gcd(a,n), so for order n we need gcd(a,n)=1.

subject: math_aa
title: Number of Generators
stem_template: How many generators does the cyclic group \\(\\mathbb{Z}_{{n}}\\) have?
mark_scheme_template: The number of generators of \\(\\mathbb{Z}_n\\) equals \\(\\phi(n)\\), Euler's totient of \\(n\\) = count of integers in \\(\\{1,\\ldots,n-1\\}\\) coprime to \\(n\\).
hints: Generators are elements with gcd(a,n) = 1.
Count them using Euler's totient function φ(n).

subject: math_aa
title: Subgroups of a Cyclic Group
stem_template: Let \\(G=\\mathbb{Z}_{{n}}\\). (a) List all subgroups of \\(G\\). (b) For each divisor \\(d\\) of \\({{n}}\\), find the unique subgroup of order \\(d\\).

parts.label: a
parts.answer_template: One subgroup of order d for each divisor d of n

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: Subgroup of order d is generated by n/d

mark_scheme_template: For \\(\\mathbb{Z}_n\\), the subgroups are in bijection with divisors of \\(n\\). The subgroup of order \\(d\\) (where \\(d\\mid n\\)) is generated by \\(\\frac{n}{d}\\), i.e., \\(H_d=\\langle n/d\\rangle\\).
hints: Every cyclic group has exactly one subgroup of each order dividing the group order.
The subgroup of order d in Zₙ is generated by n/d.

subject: math_aa
title: Cycle Notation Composition
stem_template: Let \\(\\sigma=(1\\;2\\;3)\\) and \\(\\tau=(1\\;2)\\) be permutations in \\(S_3\\). What is \\(\\sigma\\tau\\) (apply \\(\\tau\\) first)?
mark_scheme_template: Applying \\(\\tau=(12)\\) first: \\(1\\to2, 2\\to1, 3\\to3\\). Then \\(\\sigma=(123)\\): \\(2\\to3, 1\\to2, 3\\to3\\). Composition: \\(1\\to2\\to3\\), \\(2\\to1\\to2\\), \\(3\\to3\\to1\\). Wait — track: \\(1\\xrightarrow{\\tau}2\\xrightarrow{\\sigma}3\\), \\(2\\xrightarrow{\\tau}1\\xrightarrow{\\sigma}2\\), \\(3\\xrightarrow{\\tau}3\\xrightarrow{\\sigma}1\\). So \\(\\sigma\\tau=(1\\;3)\\).
hints: Apply the rightmost permutation first.
Track where each element goes under τ, then σ.

subject: math_aa
title: Order of a Permutation
stem_template: Find the order of the permutation \\(\\sigma=(1\\;2\\;3)(4\\;5)\\) in \\(S_5\\).
mark_scheme_template: The order of a permutation is the lcm of the lengths of its disjoint cycles. \\(\\text{lcm}(3,2)=6\\).
hints: Write the permutation as disjoint cycles.
The order = lcm of cycle lengths.

subject: math_aa
title: Decomposition into Transpositions
stem_template: Write the permutation \\(\\sigma=(1\\;2\\;3\\;4\\;5)\\) as a product of transpositions and determine whether it is even or odd.
mark_scheme_template: A \\(k\\)-cycle can be written as \\(k-1\\) transpositions: \\((1\;2\;3\;4\;5)=(15)(14)(13)(12)\\), which is a product of 4 transpositions. Since 4 is even, \\(\\sigma\\) is an even permutation.
hints: A k-cycle decomposes into k-1 transpositions.
Even permutation ↔ even number of transpositions.

subject: math_aa
title: Lagrange's Theorem
stem_template: A group \\(G\\) has order \\({{n}}\\). According to Lagrange's theorem, which of the following could be the order of a subgroup of \\(G\\)?
mark_scheme_template: Lagrange's Theorem: the order of any subgroup of \\(G\\) must divide \\(|G|={{n}}\\). So subgroup orders are exactly the divisors of \\({{n}}\\).
hints: Lagrange: |H| divides |G| for any subgroup H.
Possible orders are divisors of |G|.

subject: math_aa
title: Number of Cosets
stem_template: Group \\(G\\) has order \\({{g}}\\) and \\(H\\) is a subgroup of order \\({{h}}\\). How many distinct left cosets of \\(H\\) in \\(G\\) are there?
mark_scheme_template: By Lagrange's theorem, the index \\([G:H]=\\dfrac{|G|}{|H|}=\\dfrac{{{g}}}{{{h}}}\\) equals the number of distinct left cosets.
hints: Index [G:H] = |G|/|H|.
This equals the number of distinct left (or right) cosets.

subject: math_aa
title: Finding and Listing Cosets
stem_template: Let \\(G=(\\mathbb{Z}_{{{n}}}, +)\\) and \\(H=\\langle {{k}} \\rangle\\), the subgroup generated by \\({{k}}\\). (a) List the elements of \\(H\\). (b) Find all distinct left cosets of \\(H\\) in \\(G\\). (c) Verify they partition \\(G\\).

parts.label: c
parts.answer_template: Cosets are disjoint and their union is G

mark_scheme_template: (a) \\(H=\\{0, {{k}}, 2{{k}}, \\ldots\\}\\pmod{{{n}}}\\). (b) Left cosets are \\(a+H\\) for \\(a=0,1,\\ldots,{{k}}-1\\). (c) Cosets are disjoint and cover \\(\\mathbb{Z}_{{{n}}}\\), forming a partition.
hints: The subgroup H consists of all multiples of k mod n.
Each coset a+H = {a+h : h ∈ H}.

subject: math_aa
title: Homomorphism Property
stem_template: A function \\(\\phi: G\\to H\\) is a group homomorphism if and only if:
answer_template: φ(a★b) = φ(a)⊕φ(b) for all a, b ∈ G.
distractor_templates: φ maps the identity of G to the identity of G.
mark_scheme_template: A homomorphism preserves the group operation: \\(\\phi(a\\star b)=\\phi(a)\\oplus\\phi(b)\\). This must hold for all \\(a,b\\in G\\). Bijectivity makes it an isomorphism.

options_template.text: \\(\\phi\\) maps the identity of \\(G\\) to the identity of \\(G\\).

hints: A homomorphism preserves group structure.
The operation in G and in H may be different symbols.

subject: math_aa
title: Kernel of a Homomorphism
stem_template: Let \\(\\phi: \\mathbb{Z}_{{{n}}}\\to\\mathbb{Z}_{{{m}}}\\) be defined by \\(\\phi(x)=x\\bmod{{m}}\\). How many elements are in \\(\\ker(\\phi)\\)?
mark_scheme_template: \\(\\ker(\\phi)=\\{x\\in\\mathbb{Z}_n:\\phi(x)=0\\}=\\{x: m\\mid x\\}\\) in \\(\\mathbb{Z}_n\\). This has \\(n/m\\) elements.
hints: ker(φ) = {x : φ(x) = 0_H}.
Count multiples of m in Zₙ.

subject: math_aa
title: Proving an Isomorphism
stem_template: Define \\(\\phi: (\\mathbb{R}, +)\\to(\\mathbb{R}^+, \\times)\\) by \\(\\phi(x)=e^x\\). (a) Show \\(\\phi\\) is a group homomorphism. (b) Show \\(\\phi\\) is bijective. (c) Conclude that \\((\\mathbb{R},+)\\cong(\\mathbb{R}^+,\\times)\\).

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: Injective: e^x = e^y ⇒ x=y. Surjective: for y>0, x=ln(y) is a preimage

mark_scheme_template: (a) \\(\\phi(x+y)=e^{x+y}=e^x\\cdot e^y=\\phi(x)\\cdot\\phi(y)\\). ✓ (b) Injective: \\(e^x=e^y\\Rightarrow x=y\\). Surjective: any \\(y>0\\), take \\(x=\\ln y\\). (c) Bijective homomorphism \\(\\Rightarrow\\) isomorphism.
hints: A homomorphism converts + to ×.
A bijective homomorphism is an isomorphism.

subject: math_aa
title: Convergence of a Sequence
stem_template: Determine the limit of the sequence \\(a_n = \\dfrac{{{a}}n + {{b}}}{{{c}}n - {{d}}}\\) as \\(n\\to\\infty\\).
mark_scheme_template: Divide numerator and denominator by \\(n\\): \\(\\lim_{n\\to\\infty}\\dfrac{{{a}}+{{b}}/n}{{{c}}-{{d}}/n}=\\dfrac{{{a}}}{{{c}}}\\).
hints: Divide every term by the highest power of n.
Terms like b/n → 0 as n → ∞.

subject: math_aa
title: Squeeze Theorem Application
stem_template: Given \\(\\dfrac{-1}{n} \\leq a_n \\leq \\dfrac{1}{n}\\) for all \\(n\\geq 1\\). What is \\(\\lim_{n\\to\\infty} a_n\\)?
mark_scheme_template: Both \\(-\\frac{1}{n}\\to 0\\) and \\(\\frac{1}{n}\\to 0\\) as \\(n\\to\\infty\\). By the Squeeze Theorem, \\(a_n\\to 0\\).
hints: Apply the Squeeze (Sandwich) Theorem.
If g(n) ≤ aₙ ≤ h(n) and g,h → L, then aₙ → L.

subject: math_aa
title: Limit of Geometric Sequence
stem_template: Find the limit of \\(a_n = {{a}} \\cdot \\left(\\dfrac{1}{{{r}}}\\right)^n\\) as \\(n\\to\\infty\\).
mark_scheme_template: Since \\(\\left|\\frac{1}{{{r}}}\\right| = \\frac{1}{{{r}}} < 1\\), we have \\(\\left(\\frac{1}{{{r}}}\\right)^n \\to 0\\). Therefore \\(a_n = {{a}}\\cdot\\left(\\frac{1}{{{r}}}\\right)^n\\to 0\\).
hints: A geometric sequence with |r| < 1 converges to 0.
Multiplying by a constant does not change the limit.

subject: math_aa
title: Formal ε-N Definition of Convergence
stem_template: Using the formal definition of a limit, prove that \\(\\lim_{n\\to\\infty} \\dfrac{1}{n} = 0\\).

parts.label: a
parts.answer_template: State the definition: for every ε>0, ∃N such that n>N ⟹ |aₙ - 0| < ε

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: Given ε>0, choose N > 1/ε. Then n>N ⟹ 1/n < 1/N < ε

mark_scheme_template: Let \\(\\varepsilon>0\\). Choose \\(N\\in\\mathbb{N}\\) with \\(N>\\frac{1}{\\varepsilon}\\) (by Archimedean property). Then for \\(n>N\\): \\(\\left|\\frac{1}{n}-0\\right|=\\frac{1}{n}<\\frac{1}{N}<\\varepsilon\\). ✓
hints: The ε-N definition requires choosing N in terms of ε.
Note |1/n - 0| = 1/n < ε when n > 1/ε.

subject: math_aa
title: Ratio Test for Convergence
stem_template: Apply the ratio test to \\(\\sum_{n=1}^\\infty \\dfrac{{{a}}^n}{n!}\\). What does the ratio test conclude?
mark_scheme_template: \\(L=\\lim_{n\\to\\infty}\\left|\\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\\right|=\\lim_{n\\to\\infty}\\frac{{{a}}}{n+1}=0<1\\). So the series converges absolutely by the ratio test.
hints: Compute |a_{n+1}/a_n| and take the limit.
If L < 1, the series converges absolutely.

subject: math_aa
title: Harmonic Series Divergence
stem_template: Which test most directly shows that \\(\\sum_{n=1}^\\infty \\dfrac{1}{n}\\) diverges?
distractor_templates: The nth term test (1/n → 0, so test is inconclusive).
mark_scheme_template: The integral test: \\(\\int_1^\\infty \\frac{dx}{x}=\\ln x\\Big|_1^\\infty=\\infty\\). Since the integral diverges, so does \\(\\sum 1/n\\). (Cauchy condensation or Cauchy's grouping also work.)
hints: The nth term test cannot prove divergence when aₙ → 0.
The integral test connects series and integral convergence.

subject: math_aa
title: Sum of a Convergent Series
stem_template: Find the sum \\(\\displaystyle\\sum_{n=0}^{\\infty} {{a}} \\cdot \\left(\\frac{1}{{{r}}}\\right)^n\\).
mark_scheme_template: Geometric series with first term \\({{a}}\\) and common ratio \\(\\frac{1}{{{r}}}\\). Sum \\(=\\dfrac{{{a}}}{1-1/{{r}}}=\\dfrac{{{a}}{{r}}}{{{r}}-1}\\).
hints: Use the geometric series formula S = a/(1-r) for |r| < 1.
The common ratio here is 1/r.

subject: math_aa
title: Applying Multiple Convergence Tests
stem_template: Determine whether \\(\\sum_{n=1}^{\\infty} \\dfrac{{{a}}n+1}{{{b}}n^3+{{c}}}\\) converges or diverges. State the test used.

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: Apply limit comparison test, compute L = a/b, conclude convergence

mark_scheme_template: Compare with \\(\\sum \\frac{1}{n^2}\\). Limit comparison: \\(L=\\lim_{n\\to\\infty}\\dfrac{({{a}}n+1)/({{b}}n^3+{{c}})}{1/n^2}=\\lim\\dfrac{{{a}}n^3+n^2}{{{b}}n^3+{{c}}}=\\dfrac{{{a}}}{{{b}}}>0\\). Since \\(\\sum 1/n^2\\) converges and \\(L\\) is finite and positive, the series converges.
hints: For large n, the dominant term in numerator is an, denominator is bn³.
Use limit comparison with Σ 1/n².

subject: math_aa
title: Continuity at a Point
stem_template: The function \\(f(x) = \\begin{cases} {{a}}x+{{b}} & x<{{c}} \\\\ {{d}} & x={{c}} \\\\ {{e}}x^2 & x>{{c}} \\end{cases}\\) is continuous at \\(x={{c}}\\) if and only if all three values agree. What must \\(d\\) equal?
mark_scheme_template: Continuity requires \\(f(c^-)=f(c)=f(c^+)\\). Left limit: \\({{a}}\\cdot{{c}}+{{b}}\\). Right limit: \\({{e}}\\cdot{{c}}^2\\). For continuity all three equal \\(d\\). Both limits must also agree, so \\(d={{a}}{{c}}+{{b}}\\) (assuming the left limit is the required value).
hints: Continuity at c requires: left limit = f(c) = right limit.
Compute the limit from each side.

subject: math_aa
title: Mean Value Theorem
stem_template: The Mean Value Theorem guarantees a \\(c\\in(a,b)\\) such that \\(f'(c)=\\dfrac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}\\), provided:
answer_template: f is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b).
mark_scheme_template: The MVT requires \\(f\\) continuous on \\([a,b]\\) and differentiable on \\((a,b)\\). Note: Rolle's Theorem is the special case where \\(f(a)=f(b)\\).

options_template.text: \\(f\\) is continuous on \\([a,b]\\) and differentiable on \\((a,b)\\).

hints: Continuity on the closed interval and differentiability on the open interval are both required.
Differentiability on a closed interval is stronger than needed.

subject: math_aa
title: Intermediate Value Theorem Application
stem_template: Show that \\(f(x) = x^3 - {{a}}x - {{b}}\\) has a root in the interval \\(({{c}}, {{d}})\\), where \\({{c}}<{{d}}\\).

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: Apply IVT: f continuous, sign change ⟹ root exists

mark_scheme_template: \\(f\\) is a polynomial, hence continuous on \\([{{c}},{{d}}]\\). Compute \\(f({{c}})={{c}}^3-{{a}}\\cdot{{c}}-{{b}}\\) and \\(f({{d}})={{d}}^3-{{a}}\\cdot{{d}}-{{b}}\\). If one is negative and the other positive, the IVT guarantees a root in \\(({{c}},{{d}})\\).
hints: Polynomials are continuous everywhere.
Apply IVT: if f(c) and f(d) have opposite signs, there's a root between them.

subject: math_aa
title: Riemann Sum Interpretation
stem_template: The left Riemann sum for \\(\\int_{{a}}^{{b}} f(x)\\,dx\\) with \\(n={{n}}\\) subintervals uses sample points:
answer_template: x_i = a + i·Δx for i = 0, 1, ..., n-1, where Δx = (b-a)/n.
distractor_templates: x_i = a + (i+1)·Δx for i = 0, ..., n-1 (right endpoints).
x_i = a + i·(b-a) for i = 1, ..., n.
mark_scheme_template: The left Riemann sum uses left endpoints: \\(x_i=a+i\\Delta x\\) for \\(i=0,1,\\ldots,n-1\\), where \\(\\Delta x=\\frac{b-a}{n}\\).
hints: Left Riemann sum uses the left endpoint of each subinterval.
The subinterval width is Δx = (b-a)/n.

subject: math_aa
title: Improper Integral Convergence
stem_template: Determine whether \\(\\displaystyle\\int_1^{\\infty} \\dfrac{1}{x^{{{p}}}}\\,dx\\) converges or diverges, and if it converges, find its value.
hints: Compute the antiderivative and take the limit as the upper bound → ∞.
For p > 1, x^(1-p) → 0 as x → ∞.

subject: math_aa
title: Improper Integral with Discontinuity
stem_template: Evaluate \\(\\displaystyle\\int_0^1 \\dfrac{1}{x^{{{q}}}}\\,dx\\), where \\(0 < {{q}} < 1\\).

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: Integral from ε to 1, take limit as ε→0⁺, get 1/(1-q)

mark_scheme_template: \\(\\int_\\varepsilon^1 x^{-{{q}}}\\,dx=\\left[\\frac{x^{1-{{q}}}}{1-{{q}}}\\right]_\\varepsilon^1=\\frac{1-\\varepsilon^{1-{{q}}}}{1-{{q}}}\\). As \\(\\varepsilon\\to 0^+\\): since \\(1-{{q}}>0\\), \\(\\varepsilon^{1-{{q}}}\\to 0\\). Converges to \\(\\frac{1}{1-{{q}}}\\).
hints: This integral has a singularity at x = 0.
Define as lim_{ε→0⁺} ∫_ε^1 x^(-q) dx.

subject: math_aa
title: Separable Differential Equation
stem_template: The differential equation \\(\\dfrac{dy}{dx} = {{a}}xy\\) is separable. Which gives the general solution?
mark_scheme_template: Separate: \\(\\frac{dy}{y}={{a}}x\\,dx\\). Integrate: \\(\\ln|y|=\\frac{{{a}}x^2}{2}+C\\). So \\(y=Ae^{{{a}}x^2/2}\\) where \\(A=\\pm e^C\\).
hints: Separate variables: dy/y = ax dx.
Integrate both sides.

subject: math_aa
title: Solving a First-Order Linear DE
stem_template: Solve \\(\\dfrac{dy}{dx} + {{p}}y = {{q}}\\), with initial condition \\(y(0) = {{y0}}\\).
hints: Integrating factor for y' + py = q is e^(px).
Apply the initial condition to find C.

subject: math_aa
title: Euler's Method Approximation
stem_template: Use Euler's method with step size \\(h={{h}}\\) to approximate \\(y({{x1}})\\) given \\(\\dfrac{dy}{dx}=x+y\\) and \\(y(0)={{y0}}\\).

parts.label: a
parts.answer_template: Apply Euler steps: y_{n+1} = y_n + h·f(x_n, y_n)

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: State approximate value of y at x1

mark_scheme_template: Euler: \\(y_{n+1}=y_n+h(x_n+y_n)\\). Starting at \\((0,{{y0}})\\), apply the formula until reaching \\(x={{x1}}\\). Show each step with computed values.
hints: Euler's method: y_{n+1} = yₙ + h·f(xₙ, yₙ).
Apply the step repeatedly, updating x and y each time.

subject: math_aa
title: Logistic Growth Model
stem_template: The logistic equation \\(\\dfrac{dP}{dt}={{r}}P\\left(1-\\dfrac{P}{{{K}}}\\right)\\) with \\(P(0)={{P0}}\\). (a) Separate variables and integrate to find \\(P(t)\\). (b) Find the limiting population.
mark_scheme_template: Separate: \\(\\frac{dP}{P(1-P/K)}=r\\,dt\\). Partial fractions: \\(\\frac{1}{P}+\\frac{1}{K-P}\\). Integrate to get logistic solution \\(P(t)=\\frac{K}{1+\\frac{K-P_0}{P_0}e^{-rt}}\\). As \\(t\\to\\infty\\), \\(P\\to K\\).
hints: Use partial fractions on the left side.
The carrying capacity K is the long-term limit.

subject: math_aa
title: Maclaurin Series for e^x
stem_template: The Maclaurin series for \\(e^x\\) is \\(\\sum_{n=0}^{\\infty} \\dfrac{x^n}{n!}\\). Using this, write down the first four non-zero terms of the Maclaurin series for \\(e^{{{a}}x}\\).
distractor_templates: 1 + x + x²/2 + x³/6  (wrong: didn't substitute ax)
hints: Replace x with ax in the standard series for e^x.
Each term involves (ax)^n / n!.

subject: math_aa
title: Taylor Series Approximation
stem_template: Using the Maclaurin series for \\(\\sin x\\) up to the \\(x^3\\) term, approximate \\(\\sin(0.{{d}})\\) to 4 decimal places.
hints: The Maclaurin series for sin x is x - x³/3! + x⁵/5! - ...
For small x, truncating after the x³ term gives a good approximation.

subject: math_aa
title: Deriving a Maclaurin Series from First Principles
stem_template: Find the Maclaurin series for \\(f(x)=\\ln(1+{{a}}x)\\) up to and including the term in \\(x^4\\). State the radius of convergence.
mark_scheme_template: Differentiate repeatedly or use \\(\\ln(1+u)=u-\\frac{u^2}{2}+\\frac{u^3}{3}-\\cdots\\) with \\(u={{a}}x\\): \\({{a}}x-\\frac{{{a}}^2x^2}{2}+\\frac{{{a}}^3x^3}{3}-\\frac{{{a}}^4x^4}{4}+\\cdots\\). Radius of convergence \\(R=\\frac{1}{{{a}}}\\) (ratio test).
hints: Use the known series for ln(1+u) and substitute u = ax.
The ratio test gives radius of convergence 1/a.

subject: math_aa
title: Radius of Convergence via Ratio Test
stem_template: Find the radius of convergence of the power series \\(\\displaystyle\\sum_{n=0}^{\\infty} \\dfrac{({{a}}x)^n}{n^{{k}}}\\).
mark_scheme_template: Ratio test: \\(L=\\lim\\left|\\frac{({{a}}x)^{n+1}/(n+1)^{{k}}}{({{a}}x)^n/n^{{k}}}\\right|=|{{a}}x|\\cdot\\lim\\left(\\frac{n}{n+1}\\right)^{{k}}=|{{a}}x|\\). Converges iff \\(L<1\\), i.e., \\(|x|<\\frac{1}{{{a}}}\\). Radius of convergence \\(R=\\frac{1}{{{a}}}\\).
hints: Apply the ratio test to the general term.
The limit of (n/(n+1))^k as n→∞ is 1.

subject: math_aa
title: l'Hôpital's Rule Application
stem_template: Evaluate \\(\\displaystyle\\lim_{x\\to 0} \\dfrac{\\sin({{a}}x)}{{{b}}x}\\) using l'Hôpital's rule.
mark_scheme_template: Form is \\(\\frac{0}{0}\\). Apply l'Hôpital: \\(\\lim_{x\\to 0}\\frac{{{a}}\\cos({{a}}x)}{{{b}}}=\\frac{{{a}}}{{{b}}}\\).
hints: Check the form is 0/0 or ∞/∞ before applying l'Hôpital.
Differentiate numerator and denominator separately.

subject: math_aa
title: l'Hôpital with Exponential
stem_template: Evaluate \\(\\displaystyle\\lim_{x\\to\\infty} \\dfrac{x^{{{n}}}}{e^x}\\).
mark_scheme_template: Apply l'Hôpital \\({{n}}\\) times: the numerator becomes \\({{n}}!\\) (constant) and the denominator remains \\(e^x\\to\\infty\\). So the limit is \\(0\\). Exponential grows faster than any polynomial.
hints: Apply l'Hôpital's rule n times.
Exponential growth always dominates polynomial growth.

subject: math_aa
title: Limit via Maclaurin Series
stem_template: Evaluate \\(\\displaystyle\\lim_{x\\to 0} \\dfrac{e^{{{a}}x}-1-{{a}}x}{x^2}\\) using Maclaurin series.
hints: Expand e^(ax) as a Maclaurin series.
After cancellation, the limit becomes the coefficient of x².

subject: math_aa
title: Indeterminate Form 1^∞
stem_template: Evaluate \\(\\displaystyle\\lim_{x\\to\\infty} \\left(1+\\dfrac{{{a}}}{x}\\right)^x\\).

parts.label: a
parts.answer_template: Write as exp(x ln(1 + a/x)) and find lim of exponent

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: lim x·ln(1+a/x) = a via l'Hôpital, so answer is e^a

mark_scheme_template: Let \\(L=\\lim x\\ln(1+a/x)\\). As \\(x\\to\\infty\\), this is \\(\\infty\\cdot 0\\) form. Rewrite: \\(\\frac{\\ln(1+a/x)}{1/x}\\to\\frac{0}{0}\\). l'Hôpital: differentiate numerator and denominator with respect to \\(x\\). Result: \\(L={{a}}\\). So limit \\(=e^{{{a}}}\\).
hints: Write the expression as e^(x·ln(1+a/x)).
The exponent has indeterminate form ∞·0; rewrite as a fraction for l'Hôpital.

subject: math_aa
title: Pigeonhole Principle Basic
stem_template: There are \\({{n}}\\) students and \\({{k}}\\) possible birthdays in a year. What is the minimum number of students needed to guarantee at least two share a birthday month (12 months)?
mark_scheme_template: By the Pigeonhole Principle: with 12 pigeonholes (months) and 13 pigeons (students), at least two must share the same month. So the answer is \\(12+1=13\\).
hints: With n pigeonholes, you need n+1 pigeons to guarantee a collision.
12 months → need 13 students.

subject: math_aa
title: Strong Induction vs Weak Induction
stem_template: Strong induction differs from weak induction in that the inductive step assumes:
answer_template: The statement holds for ALL k ≤ n (not just k = n) to prove it for n+1.
distractor_templates: The statement holds only for k = n to prove it for n+1.
The base case covers more values.
There is no inductive hypothesis — it is proved directly.
mark_scheme_template: In strong (complete) induction, the inductive hypothesis assumes \\(P(k)\\) holds for ALL \\(k\\leq n\\) (not just \\(n\\)), and uses this to prove \\(P(n+1)\\). This is useful when \\(P(n+1)\\) depends on earlier cases, not just the immediate predecessor.

options_template.text: The statement holds for ALL \\(k\\leq n\\) to prove it for \\(n+1\\).
The statement holds only for \\(k=n\\) to prove it for \\(n+1\\).
The base case covers more values.
There is no inductive hypothesis — it is proved directly.

hints: In weak induction, we assume P(n) to prove P(n+1).
In strong induction, we assume P(1), P(2), ..., P(n) all hold.

subject: math_aa
title: Strong Induction Proof
stem_template: Use strong induction to prove that every integer \\(n\\geq 2\\) can be written as a product of prime numbers.

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: Inductive step: assume true for all 2 ≤ k ≤ n. If n+1 prime, done. If composite, n+1=ab with 2≤a,b≤n, apply hypothesis to each

mark_scheme_template: Base case: \\(n=2\\) is prime. ✓ Inductive step: Assume every integer \\(2\\leq k\\leq n\\) is a product of primes. Consider \\(n+1\\): if prime, done. If composite, \\(n+1=ab\\) with \\(2\\leq a,b\\leq n\\). By IH, both \\(a\\) and \\(b\\) are products of primes, so \\(n+1=ab\\) is also a product of primes. ✓
hints: This requires strong induction because the factors of n+1 may be far from n.
A composite number n+1 = ab with a, b < n+1 — apply the inductive hypothesis to a and b.

subject: math_aa
title: Pigeonhole Application
stem_template: Among any \\({{n}}\\) integers, show that at least two have the same remainder when divided by \\({{n}}-1\\).

parts.label: a
parts.answer_template: Identify the pigeonholes: remainders 0, 1, ..., n-2 (n-1 choices)

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: n integers, n-1 possible remainders → by PHP, two integers share a remainder

mark_scheme_template: When dividing by \\({{n}}-1\\), remainders lie in \\(\\{0,1,\\ldots,{{n}}-2\\}\\), giving \\({{n}}-1\\) possible values. With \\({{n}}\\) integers, by the Pigeonhole Principle, at least two must have the same remainder. ✓
hints: The 'pigeonholes' are the possible remainders mod (n-1).
n-1 holes, n pigeons → at least one hole has ≥ 2 pigeons.

subject: math_aa
title: GCD via Euclidean Algorithm
stem_template: Find \\(\\gcd({{a}}, {{b}})\\) using the Euclidean algorithm.
mark_scheme_template: Apply the Euclidean algorithm: \\({{a}}={{b}}\\cdot q_1+r_1\\), \\({{b}}=r_1\\cdot q_2+r_2\\), etc., until remainder is 0. The last non-zero remainder is \\(\\gcd({{a}},{{b}})\\).

options_template.text: The last non-zero remainder in the Euclidean algorithm.

hints: Euclidean algorithm: gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b).
Continue until remainder = 0.

subject: math_aa
title: Computing LCM from GCD
mark_scheme_template: \\(\\text{lcm}(a,b)=\\dfrac{ab}{\\gcd(a,b)}=\\dfrac{{{a}}\\cdot{{b}}}{{{d}}}\\).
hints: Use lcm(a,b) × gcd(a,b) = a × b.
Rearrange to find lcm.

subject: math_aa
title: Infinitely Many Primes
stem_template: Prove that there are infinitely many prime numbers. (Euclid's proof)

parts.label: a
parts.answer_template: Assume finite set of primes p₁,...,pₙ, form N = p₁p₂...pₙ + 1

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: N has a prime factor not in the list — contradiction

mark_scheme_template: Suppose for contradiction there are finitely many primes \\(p_1,\\ldots,p_n\\). Let \\(N=p_1p_2\\cdots p_n+1\\). Then \\(N>1\\) has a prime factor \\(p\\). But \\(p\\nmid N - p_1\\cdots p_n=1\\), so \\(p\\notin\\{p_1,\\ldots,p_n\\}\\). Contradiction. ✓
hints: Assume finitely many primes and derive a contradiction.
The product + 1 cannot be divisible by any of the listed primes.

subject: math_aa
title: Existence of Solutions to Linear Diophantine Equation
stem_template: The equation \\({{a}}x + {{b}}y = {{c}}\\) has integer solutions if and only if:
mark_scheme_template: The linear Diophantine equation \\(ax+by=c\\) has integer solutions iff \\(\\gcd(a,b)\\mid c\\).
hints: This is Bezout's identity.
Integer solutions exist iff gcd(a,b) | c.

subject: math_aa
title: Solving a Linear Diophantine Equation
stem_template: Find all integer solutions to \\({{a}}x + {{b}}y = \\gcd({{a}},{{b}})\\).
answer_template: x = x0 + (b/d)*t, y = y0 - (a/d)*t for integer t, where (x0,y0) is a particular solution from extended Euclidean algorithm
mark_scheme_template: Use the extended Euclidean algorithm to find a particular solution \\((x_0,y_0)\\). General solution: \\(x=x_0+\\frac{b}{d}t\\), \\(y=y_0-\\frac{a}{d}t\\), \\(t\\in\\mathbb{Z}\\), where \\(d=\\gcd(a,b)\\).
hints: Use the extended Euclidean algorithm to find a particular solution.
The general solution adds multiples of b/d and -a/d.

subject: math_aa
title: Word Problem with Diophantine Equation
stem_template: A shopkeeper sells apples for \\({{p1}}\\) pence and oranges for \\({{p2}}\\) pence. A customer spends exactly \\({{total}}\\) pence on a mixture. Find all non-negative integer solutions.

hints: Set up the Diophantine equation from the problem.
After finding the general solution, apply the constraint x, y ≥ 0.

subject: math_aa
title: Modular Arithmetic Calculation
stem_template: Find the value of \\({{a}}^{{{n}}} \\pmod{{{m}}}\\), where \\({{m}}\\) is prime.
mark_scheme_template: Compute \\({{a}}^{{{n}}}\\pmod{{{m}}}\\) by successive squaring or repeated multiplication. Reduce at each step.
hints: Reduce after each multiplication to keep numbers small.
Use the property (ab) mod m = ((a mod m)(b mod m)) mod m.

subject: math_aa
title: Chinese Remainder Theorem
stem_template: Find the smallest positive integer \\(x\\) such that \\(x\\equiv{{r1}}\\pmod{{{m1}}}\\) and \\(x\\equiv{{r2}}\\pmod{{{m2}}}\\), where \\(\\gcd({{m1}},{{m2}})=1\\).
mark_scheme_template: By CRT, since \\(\\gcd({{m1}},{{m2}})=1\\), a unique solution exists modulo \\({{m1}}\\cdot{{m2}}\\). Find it by substitution or using \\(x={{r1}}+{{m1}}t\\) and solving \\({{r1}}+{{m1}}t\\equiv{{r2}}\\pmod{{{m2}}}\\).

Solve for t mod m2 to find the solution.

subject: math_aa
title: System of Congruences
stem_template: Solve the system: \\(x\\equiv{{a}}\\pmod{{{m1}}}\\), \\(x\\equiv{{b}}\\pmod{{{m2}}}\\), \\(x\\equiv{{c}}\\pmod{{{m3}}}\\), where \\({{m1}}, {{m2}}, {{m3}}\\) are pairwise coprime.

parts.label: a
parts.answer_template: Combine first two congruences using CRT

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: Combine result with third congruence, state solution mod m1·m2·m3

hints: Apply CRT step by step.
The final solution is unique modulo m1·m2·m3.

subject: math_aa

subject: math_aa

parts.answer_template: Add in given base, carrying correctly

Convert to base 10 to verify your answer.

subject: math_aa
title: Fermat's Little Theorem
stem_template: By Fermat's Little Theorem, if \\(p={{p}}\\) is prime and \\(\\gcd(a,p)=1\\), then \\(a^{{{pm1}}}\\equiv\\;?\\;\\pmod{{{p}}}\\).

hints: Fermat's Little Theorem: a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p) for prime p with gcd(a,p)=1.
This has important applications in computing large powers modulo a prime.

subject: math_aa
title: Large Power via Fermat's Little Theorem
stem_template: Compute \\({{a}}^{{{n}}} \\pmod{{{p}}}\\) where \\(p={{p}}\\) is prime, using Fermat's Little Theorem.
hints: Use a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p) to reduce the exponent.
Compute n mod (p-1) first, then raise a to that reduced power.

subject: math_aa
title: Wilson's Theorem and Primality
stem_template: State and prove Wilson's Theorem: \\((p-1)!\\equiv -1\\pmod{p}\\) for prime \\(p\\). Verify for \\(p={{p}}\\).

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: Pair each a with its inverse mod p, leaving 1 and p-1 unpaired

mark_scheme_template: For prime \\(p\\), each \\(a\\in\\{2,\\ldots,p-2\\}\\) can be paired with its unique inverse \\(a^{-1}\\neq a\\) in \\(\\{1,\\ldots,p-1\\}\\). Each pair multiplies to 1. The elements 1 and \\(p-1\\) are self-inverse. So \\((p-1)!=1\\cdot(p-1)\\cdot(\\text{pairs})\\equiv(p-1)\\equiv -1\\pmod{p}\\). Verify: \\(({{p}}-1)!=\\ldots\\equiv -1\\pmod{{{p}}}\\).
hints: Each element a ≠ 1, p-1 in Z_p* pairs with its distinct inverse.
Only 1 and p-1 are their own inverses mod p.

subject: math_aa
title: Handshaking Lemma
stem_template: A graph has \\({{v}}\\) vertices. The sum of all vertex degrees is \\({{s}}\\). How many edges does the graph have?
mark_scheme_template: Handshaking Lemma: \\(\\sum_{v} \\deg(v) = 2|E|\\). So \\(|E|=\\dfrac{{{s}}}{2}\\).
hints: The Handshaking Lemma: Σdeg(v) = 2|E|.
Each edge contributes 2 to the sum of degrees.

subject: math_aa
title: Graph Degree Sequence
stem_template: Which degree sequence is NOT possible for a simple graph?
answer_template: 3, 3, 3, 1 (sum = 10, which is even, but check graph exists).

hints: Sum of all degrees must be even.
Check Σdeg for each option.

subject: math_aa
title: Complete Graph Edges
stem_template: How many edges does the complete graph \\(K_{{n}}\\) have?
mark_scheme_template: In \\(K_n\\), every vertex connects to every other vertex. Number of edges \\(=\\binom{n}{2}=\\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}=\\dfrac{{{n}}({{n}}-1)}{2}\\).
hints: Each pair of vertices forms one edge in K_n.
Use the combination formula C(n,2).

subject: math_aa
title: Graph Planarity and Euler's Formula
stem_template: A connected planar graph has \\(v={{v}}\\) vertices and \\(e={{e}}\\) edges. (a) Apply Euler's formula to find the number of faces \\(f\\). (b) For a simple planar graph, verify the inequality \\(e\\leq 3v-6\\).
mark_scheme_template: (a) Euler's formula: \\(v-e+f=2\\Rightarrow f=2+e-v=2+{{e}}-{{v}}\\). (b) For a simple planar graph, \\(e\\leq 3v-6=3\\cdot{{v}}-6\\). Check: \\({{e}}\\leq{{3v-6}}\\).
hints: Euler's formula: v - e + f = 2 for connected planar graphs.
The inequality e ≤ 3v-6 is a necessary condition for planarity.

subject: math_aa
title: Euler Circuit Existence
stem_template: A connected graph has \\({{v}}\\) vertices. Which condition guarantees an Eulerian circuit (traversing every edge exactly once and returning to start)?
distractor_templates: Exactly two vertices have odd degree.
mark_scheme_template: An Eulerian circuit exists iff the graph is connected and every vertex has even degree (Euler's theorem). An Eulerian path (not circuit) exists iff exactly two vertices have odd degree.

options_template.text: Exactly two vertices have odd degree.

hints: Eulerian circuit: all vertices have even degree.
Eulerian path (not circuit): exactly 2 vertices have odd degree.

subject: math_aa
title: Odd-Degree Vertices Count
stem_template: A graph has degree sequence \\(({{d1}}, {{d2}}, {{d3}}, {{d4}}, {{d5}})\\). How many vertices have odd degree?
mark_scheme_template: Count the odd values in the degree sequence. Note: by the Handshaking Lemma, the number of odd-degree vertices is always even.
hints: Count which of the listed degrees are odd.
The number of odd-degree vertices is always even (Handshaking Lemma).

subject: math_aa
title: Finding an Euler Path
stem_template: A graph \\(G\\) has vertices \\(A, B, C, D, E\\) with edges \\(AB, AC, BC, BD, CD, DE\\). (a) Find the degree of each vertex. (b) Determine whether an Euler path or circuit exists. (c) If an Euler path exists, write it down.

parts.answer_template: Exactly 4 odd-degree vertices — neither Euler path nor circuit

Euler path requires exactly 0 or 2 vertices of odd degree.

subject: math_aa
title: Kruskal's Algorithm Objective
stem_template: Kruskal's algorithm finds:
answer_template: A minimum spanning tree by repeatedly adding the cheapest edge that doesn't form a cycle.
distractor_templates: The shortest path between two specific vertices.
The maximum flow through a network.
A Hamiltonian cycle of minimum weight.
mark_scheme_template: Kruskal's algorithm: sort edges by weight, add each edge (cheapest first) if it doesn't create a cycle, until the tree spans all vertices. This gives a minimum spanning tree (MST).

options_template.text: A minimum spanning tree by adding cheapest edges without forming cycles.
The shortest path between two specific vertices.
The maximum flow through a network.
A Hamiltonian cycle of minimum weight.

hints: Kruskal's = greedy edge-addition for minimum spanning tree.
Avoid cycles at each step.

subject: math_aa
title: Minimum Spanning Tree Weight
stem_template: A weighted graph with {{v}} vertices has edges with weights \\({{e1}}, {{e2}}, {{e3}}, {{e4}}, {{e5}}, {{e6}}\\). Apply Kruskal's algorithm to find the total weight of the minimum spanning tree.
answer_template: Sum of the v-1 lightest non-cycle-forming edges

hints: A spanning tree with v vertices has exactly v-1 edges.
Kruskal's: sort edges, add if no cycle is formed.

subject: math_aa
title: Dijkstra's Algorithm
stem_template: Apply Dijkstra's algorithm to the weighted graph below to find the shortest path from vertex \\(A\\) to vertex \\(E\\). Edge weights: \\(AB={{w1}}\\), \\(AC={{w2}}\\), \\(BC={{w3}}\\), \\(BD={{w4}}\\), \\(CD={{w5}}\\), \\(DE={{w6}}\\), \\(CE={{w7}}\\).

parts.label: a
parts.answer_template: Step through Dijkstra's algorithm showing updated distances

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: State shortest path and its total distance

hints: Dijkstra's: always extend the shortest known partial path.
Keep a table of tentative distances, updating when shorter paths are found.

subject: math_aa
title: Chinese Postman Problem
stem_template: The Chinese Postman Problem seeks to find:
answer_template: The minimum-weight closed walk traversing every edge at least once.
distractor_templates: The longest path through the graph.
mark_scheme_template: The Chinese Postman Problem (Route Inspection) finds the minimum-weight closed walk covering every edge at least once. If the graph is Eulerian (all even degrees), the answer equals the total edge weight. Otherwise, duplicate some edges to make all vertices even-degree.

options_template.text: The minimum-weight closed walk traversing every edge at least once.
The longest path through the graph.

hints: Chinese Postman = Route Inspection Problem.
If all vertices have even degree, the answer = sum of all edge weights.

subject: math_aa
title: Chinese Postman Minimum Route
stem_template: A graph has total edge weight \\({{total}}\\). It has exactly two odd-degree vertices connected by the shortest path of weight \\({{extra}}\\). Find the minimum route for the Chinese Postman Problem.
mark_scheme_template: Two odd-degree vertices must be connected by a repeated path. The minimum extra distance is the shortest path between them (\\({{extra}}\\)). Total \\(={{total}}+{{extra}}\\).
hints: Add the shortest path between odd-degree vertices to make all degrees even.
Minimum route = total edge weight + repeated edge weight.

subject: math_aa
title: Travelling Salesman Problem — Nearest Neighbour
stem_template: A salesman must visit cities \\(A, B, C, D\\) and return to \\(A\\). Distances: \\(AB={{ab}}\\), \\(AC={{ac}}\\), \\(AD={{ad}}\\), \\(BC={{bc}}\\), \\(BD={{bd}}\\), \\(CD={{cd}}\\). (a) Apply the nearest-neighbour heuristic starting from \\(A\\). (b) Find the optimal tour by exhaustive search.

parts.label: a
parts.answer_template: Apply nearest-neighbour heuristic step by step

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: List all 3 distinct tours (n-1)!/2 = 3, find minimum

mark_scheme_template: (a) Nearest neighbour from A: at each step, go to the nearest unvisited city. Record the route and total distance. (b) The \\(3\\) distinct tours are \\(A\\to B\\to C\\to D\\to A\\), \\(A\\to B\\to D\\to C\\to A\\), \\(A\\to C\\to B\\to D\\to A\\). Calculate each and identify the minimum.
hints: With 4 cities, there are (4-1)!/2 = 3 distinct tours.
Compare all 3 to find the optimal solution.

subject: math_aa
title: Linear Recurrence Characteristic Equation
stem_template: The recurrence relation \\(a_n = {{p}}a_{n-1} + {{q}}a_{n-2}\\) has characteristic equation:
mark_scheme_template: Substitute \\(a_n=x^n\\) into the recurrence \\(x^n={{p}}x^{n-1}+{{q}}x^{n-2}\\). Divide by \\(x^{n-2}\\): \\(x^2-{{p}}x-{{q}}=0\\).
hints: Substitute a_n = x^n and divide by x^(n-2).
All terms on the same side with the leading coefficient positive.

subject: math_aa
title: Solving a Recurrence with Distinct Roots
answer_template: A·r1^n + B·r2^n where r1,r2 are roots of x²-px+q=0
mark_scheme_template: Characteristic equation \\(x^2-{{p}}x+{{q}}=0\\). Find roots \\(r_1, r_2\\). General solution: \\(a_n=Ar_1^n+Br_2^n\\). Apply initial conditions \\(a_0={{a0}}, a_1={{a1}}\\) to find \\(A\\) and \\(B\\).
hints: Find the characteristic roots first.
Use the initial conditions to determine the constants A and B.

subject: math_aa
title: Recurrence with Repeated Roots
stem_template: Solve the recurrence \\(a_n = {{r}}a_{n-1} - \\dfrac{{{r}}^2}{4}a_{n-2}\\) ... Actually: Solve \\(a_n - {{twor}}a_{n-1} + {{rsq}}a_{n-2} = 0\\) (with repeated root \\(r={{r}}\\)) given \\(a_0={{a0}}\\), \\(a_1={{a1}}\\).

parts.label: c
parts.answer_template: Apply initial conditions to find A and B

hints: With a repeated root r, the general solution is (A + Bn)·r^n.
Apply both initial conditions to find A and B.

subject: math_aa
title: Non-Homogeneous Recurrence Relation

parts.label: b
parts.answer_template: Find particular solution: try a_n^(p) = B·c^n, get B = c^n/(c-p)

parts.label: c
parts.answer_template: Apply initial condition to get A

mark_scheme_template: Homogeneous solution: \\(a_n^{(h)}=A{{p}}^n\\). For particular solution, try \\(a_n^{(p)}=B{{c}}^n\\): \\(B{{c}}^n={{p}}B{{c}}^{n-1}+{{c}}^n\\Rightarrow B=\\frac{{{c}}}{{{c}}-{{p}}}\\) (if \\({{c}}\\neq{{p}}\\)). General solution: \\(a_n=A{{p}}^n+\\frac{{{c}}^{n+1}}{{{c}}-{{p}}}\\). Apply \\(a_0={{a0}}\\) to find \\(A\\).
hints: Separate into homogeneous and particular solutions.
For the particular solution, try a_n = B·c^n (if c ≠ p).

subject: math_aa
title: Complex Number Properties
stem_template: Let \(z = a + bi\). Which of the following statements are TRUE? Select ALL that apply.

options_template.text: The conjugate of \(z\) is \(\bar{z} = a - bi\)

mark_scheme_template: TRUE: |z|=√(a²+b²); conjugate ā=a-bi; z·ā=a²+b²; i⁴=1. FALSE: z+ā=2a (real part × 2), not 2bi.
hints: Conjugate: change sign of imaginary part.
z × z̄ is always real.

subject: math_aa
title: Integration Techniques
stem_template: Which of the following integrals can be solved directly using the substitution method \(u = g(x)\)? Select ALL that apply.
mark_scheme_template: Substitution works when the integrand contains a function and its derivative: 2x·e^(x²) (u=x²); cos(3x) (u=3x); 2x/(x²+1) (u=x²+1). Integration by parts needed for x²eˣ and x ln x.
hints: Look for f'(x)·f(g(x)) patterns.
x²eˣ and x ln x require integration by parts.

subject: math_aa
title: Probability Concepts
stem_template: Two events \(A\) and \(B\) are mutually exclusive. Which statements MUST be true? Select ALL that apply.
mark_scheme_template: Mutually exclusive: P(A∩B)=0; P(A∪B)=P(A)+P(B); P(A|B)=0; P(A)+P(B)≤1. NOT independent (mutual exclusivity implies dependence unless one has P=0).
hints: Mutually exclusive → cannot occur together.
Independence requires P(A∩B)=P(A)·P(B).

subject: math_aa
title: Integration by Parts
stem_template: Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int x \cdot e^{{{k}}x} \, dx\) using integration by parts.

steps_template.label: Identify u and dv
steps_template.content: Let \(u = x\) and \(dv = e^{{{k}}x} \, dx\), so \(du = dx\) and \(v = \dfrac{1}{{{k}}}e^{{{k}}x}\).

steps_template.label: Apply formula
steps_template.content: Integration by parts: \(\int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du\). Write out \(\dfrac{x}{{{k}}}e^{{{k}}x} - \int \dfrac{1}{{{k}}}e^{{{k}}x} \, dx\).

steps_template.label: What is the coefficient of \(e^{{{k}}x}\) in the final answer?
steps_template.content: The final answer is \(\dfrac{x}{{{k}}}e^{{{k}}x} - \dfrac{1}{{{k}}^2}e^{{{k}}x} + C\). What is the coefficient of the second term \(e^{{{k}}x}\)?

hints: Let u=x (easy to differentiate).
The integral of e^(kx) is (1/k)e^(kx).
